skill.name: lark-drive
skill.description: Manage Lark Drive and Feishu Drive files, folders, comments, permissions, metadata, and user comment subscriptions through lark-cli workflows.
skill.summary: Use lark-cli guidance to manage Lark Drive files, comments, permissions, and subscriptions.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found that most are markdown examples, relative documentation links, placeholder URLs, and language entropy false positives. The real risk is operational: this community skill guides lark-cli network calls that can upload, download, comment on, and change access to Lark Drive content. Publish with a warning that write actions and local file transfers require explicit user confirmation and appropriate Lark permissions.

medium_findings.title: Networked Lark Drive Operations Through CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill provides lark-cli workflows for Lark Drive operations, including file upload, download, comments, permission management, metadata lookup, and event subscriptions. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can modify cloud documents and send data to Lark services, so user intent and account scope must be confirmed before write actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and API resource list directly show external service operations and document write actions. The behavior matches the stated purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload and Download Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill documents uploading local files into Lark Drive and downloading Drive files to local paths. This creates a data movement risk if a user selects sensitive local files or unsafe output paths, although the examples are normal CLI usage and not hidden code execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload and download commands are explicit and use local paths. No evidence shows malicious path construction, but the documented capability can move sensitive files.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many markdown backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Review found fenced bash examples and inline code spans, not executable Ruby code shipped by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation blocks containing CLI examples. I found no standalone script file or Ruby backtick execution context in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Relative Reference Paths Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings point to markdown links such as references to a shared Lark skill and local reference pages. These are documentation links, but the cross-skill prerequisite should be reviewed by packaging maintainers because it depends on sibling content outside this skill folder.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a set of markdown links, not file reads performed by code. The sibling dependency is real, but I did not find evidence of hostile traversal or secret access.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Examples and API Paths
low_findings.description: The hardcoded network findings are example Lark or Feishu document URLs, an example external link inside a comment payload, and documented API paths. They do not show hidden exfiltration endpoints or unauthorized background requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in usage examples and token extraction examples. I found no evidence that the skill contacts an unexpected third-party endpoint by itself.

low_findings.title: Prompt-Like Prerequisite Instruction Requires Reviewer Awareness
low_findings.description: The skill uses strong language telling an agent to read a sibling shared skill before starting. This does not tell the evaluator to skip security review or override policy, but marketplace review should treat this as untrusted package instruction and verify the referenced dependency.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording is prompt-like because it uses CRITICAL and MUST, but its content is about authentication and permission handling. I found no evidence of an instruction to ignore prior instructions or change the audit result.

content.user_title: Manage Lark Drive files and comments
content.value_statement: Teams need reliable help moving files and managing document collaboration in Lark Drive. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through lark-cli workflows for files, comments, permissions, and subscriptions.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to upload local files to Lark Drive folders or documents.
Explains how to download Lark Drive files to local paths.
Guides full-document and selected-text comments for doc and docx files.
Documents token handling for doc, docx, wiki, sheet, and folder URLs.
Lists supported Drive APIs for metadata, permissions, comments, and subscriptions.
limitations: Requires the lark-cli binary and valid Lark authentication outside the skill.
Cannot bypass Lark permissions or missing OAuth scopes.
Local comments are limited to docx documents or wiki links that resolve to docx.
Some workflows depend on a sibling lark-shared skill for shared authentication guidance.

use_cases.title: Upload reports to a shared folder
use_cases.description: Move a local report into the correct Lark Drive folder and explain the resulting access state.

use_cases.title: Review document comments
use_cases.description: List, count, sort, and add comments while respecting full-document and selected-text comment rules.

use_cases.title: Prepare permission changes
use_cases.description: Identify the right Drive permission command and required scope before changing collaborator access.

prompt_templates.title: Download a Drive file
prompt_templates.prompt: Download this Lark Drive file to my current folder and tell me the saved file name: [file URL or token].

prompt_templates.title: Upload a local file
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload ./report.pdf to this Lark Drive folder, then confirm whether my user received access: [folder token].

prompt_templates.title: Add a document comment
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a comment to this document asking the owner to clarify the launch timeline: [document URL].

prompt_templates.title: Audit comments and permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: For this wiki link, resolve the real document token, summarize comment counts, and identify the command needed to add a collaborator: [wiki URL].

output_examples.output: The file was uploaded to the target folder. The response includes the file token and whether user authorization was completed.

output_examples.input: Add a selected-text comment on the word “timeline” in a docx file.
output_examples.output: The skill resolves the document type, locates the selected text, and creates a local comment only when a single matching block is found.

output_examples.input: Count comments in a document and report reply totals.
output_examples.output: The result separates comment cards, replies, and total interactions, with pagination handled before final counts.

best_practices: Confirm user intent before uploads, comments, permission changes, subscriptions, or owner transfers.
Resolve wiki links before choosing a file token or document API.
Check required Lark scopes and current identity before changing permissions.
anti_patterns: Do not upload local files unless the user names the exact file and destination.
Do not assume a wiki URL contains the final file token.
Do not reply to resolved or full-document comments when Lark does not allow it.

faq.question: Does this skill include its own Lark credentials?
faq.answer: No. It requires lark-cli authentication and permissions configured outside the skill.

faq.question: Can it upload files from my computer?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents local file upload commands, so users should confirm each file and destination.

faq.question: Can it download files to a chosen path?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide downloads to a local output path when the user provides a file token.

faq.question: Does it support wiki links?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains that wiki links must be resolved to the real object type and token first.

faq.question: Can it add selected-text comments?
faq.answer: Yes, for docx documents or wiki links that resolve to docx documents.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it performs real cloud and local file operations.
